frameworks.name: hierarchical Bayesian model
frameworks.one_line: individual and group moral values inferred as latent variables from judgments

frameworks.name: utility calculus
frameworks.one_line: moral choice cast as weighted trade-off across dilemma features

frameworks.name: cognitive theory of moral learning
frameworks.one_line: draws on how children learn moral theories from sparse noisy data

core_question: Can a hierarchical Bayesian utility-weighting model infer individual and group moral preferences from sparse, noisy human judgments on autonomous-vehicle dilemmas?
key_assumptions: moral judgment decomposes into feature weights in a utility calculus
individuals and groups share hierarchical structure over weights
sparse noisy judgments suffice to identify latent moral values
manipulations: varied dilemma features (who is harmed, how many, group membership)
amount of data available per individual / group
key_findings: model infers individual moral values from limited Moral Machine judgments
shared group-level values recovered hierarchically across populations
model predicts difficulty of moral dilemmas from limited data
interpretations: human moral preferences can be operationalized as inferable utility weights
hierarchical Bayesian inference supports moral-decision modules for autonomous vehicles
